title: What to know about Dorema ammoniacum  (iranian galbanum) before using it medicinally
section_medicine_ailments: Dorema ammoniacum helps healing several common ailments, such as digestive issues, respiratory problems, and skin irritations. The herb has been traditionally used to treat symptoms of indigestion, bloating, and diarrhea, thanks to its antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory properties that soothe the digestive tract and alleviate cramping. Additionally, Dorema ammoniacum's expectorant qualities make it effective in relieving congestion and coughs associated with colds, bronchitis, and asthma, by loosening and expelling mucus from the airways. Furthermore, its antiseptic and antibacterial properties make it useful in treating skin conditions such as acne, eczema, and wounds, promoting faster healing and reducing inflammation.
section_medicine_properties: Dorema ammoniacum has several medicinal properties, such as anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antibacterial activities. The plant's most significant property is its ability to alleviate respiratory issues, particularly bronchitis, asthma, and chronic coughs, due to its expectorant and mucolytic effects. It also possesses antispasmodic properties that help relax the airways, reducing inflammation and congestion. Additionally, Dorema ammoniacum has been shown to exhibit antimicrobial activity against various bacterial strains, including those responsible for respiratory infections. The plant's antioxidant properties may contribute to its ability to protect against oxidative stress and cell damage, which could have implications for treating chronic diseases such as cancer and cardiovascular disease. Furthermore, the plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and skin problems, highlighting its potential therapeutic value beyond respiratory issues. Overall, the medicinal properties of Dorema ammoniacum make it a valuable herbal remedy for treating respiratory ailments and other health conditions.
section_medicine_parts: The most commonly used parts of Dorema ammoniacum for medicinal purposes are the resin, essential oil, and leaves. The resin is rich in volatile oils, including bornyl acetate, which has been shown to exhibit antibacterial and antifungal properties, making it effective against a range of microorganisms. The essential oil, extracted from the plant's leaves and stems, contains a high concentration of borneol, a compound with analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects that can help alleviate pain and reduce swelling. Additionally, the leaves themselves have been traditionally used to treat respiratory issues such as bronchitis, asthma, and coughs, due to their expectorant properties. The resin and essential oil are often used together in traditional medicine to create a poultice or compress, which is applied topically to relieve pain and reduce inflammation in conditions such as rheumatism and arthritis. Overall, the unique combination of bioactive compounds present in Dorema ammoniacum makes it a valuable medicinal plant with a range of therapeutic applications.
section_medicine_side_effects: When used improperly, Dorema ammoniacum increases the chances of experiencing side effects, such as allergic reactions, respiratory issues, and gastrointestinal problems. If consumed in excess or without proper preparation, the essential oils contained in Dorema ammoniacum can cause an adverse reaction, leading to symptoms like hives, itching, and difficulty breathing. Furthermore, improper use may also result in nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and abdominal pain as a consequence of irritant properties. In some cases, excessive consumption or topical application without proper dilution can cause skin irritation, redness, and inflammation. Additionally, if Dorema ammoniacum is combined with other substances or used to treat underlying conditions that are not properly diagnosed, it may interact with medications, worsen symptoms, or even delay healing, leading to more severe side effects. It is crucial to consult with a healthcare professional and follow established guidelines for the safe use of Dorema ammoniacum to minimize the risk of adverse reactions and maximize its potential therapeutic benefits.
section_medicine_precautions: The precautions to take before using Dorema ammoniacum medicinally are essential to ensure its safe and effective use. As a plant-based remedy, Dorema ammoniacum has been traditionally used to treat various ailments, including respiratory issues, digestive problems, and skin conditions. However, it is crucial to follow certain guidelines when utilizing this herb for medicinal purposes. Firstly, it is recommended to consult with a healthcare professional or an experienced herbalist before using Dorema ammoniacum, especially if you have any pre-existing medical conditions, are pregnant, breastfeeding, or taking medications. Secondly, the recommended dosages should be strictly adhered to, as excessive consumption can lead to adverse effects such as nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain. Additionally, it is essential to ensure proper identification of the plant, as Dorema ammoniacum can resemble other species that may not possess similar benefits or be toxic in nature. Finally, proper storage and handling practices should be followed to prevent contamination and maintain the herb's potency. By taking these precautions, individuals can safely harness the therapeutic potential of Dorema ammoniacum and reap its numerous health benefits.
section_horticulture: Dorema ammoniacum, a species of flowering plant in the carrot family, is native to the mountainous regions of Central Asia and Eastern Europe. It thrives in well-drained soil, typically found in rocky outcrops, sandy areas, and dry scrublands at elevations between 1,000 to 3,000 meters above sea level. This plant requires full sun to partial shade, with an optimal temperature range of 15°C to 25°C (59°F to 77°F) for germination and growth. Dorema ammoniacum is relatively drought-tolerant, but it still needs regular watering during its active growing period. It can tolerate some frost but may die back in extreme cold temperatures. In ideal conditions, the plant can grow up to 1 meter (3.3 feet) tall, producing umbels of small yellow flowers that bloom from June to August. Overall, Dorema ammoniacum prefers a Mediterranean climate with low rainfall and moderate winters, making it an attractive choice for gardeners in regions with similar conditions.
section_horticulture_growth: Dorema ammoniacum, a perennial herb native to the Middle East and Central Asia, requires specific growth conditions to thrive. It prefers well-draining soil with a pH range of 6.0-7.5, where it can absorb adequate moisture and nutrients. The plant thrives in full sun to partial shade, with average temperatures between 15°C to 25°C (59°F to 77°F). To promote healthy growth, Dorema ammoniacum requires an annual rainfall of at least 300 mm (12 inches), with occasional drought periods allowing the soil to dry slightly. The plant's ideal growing conditions are those characterized by a Mediterranean climate, with warm and dry summers, and cool, wet winters. Additionally, Dorema ammoniacum benefits from regular pruning, which encourages bushy growth and prevents the plant from becoming leggy or sprawling. By providing these specific growth requirements, gardeners can successfully cultivate this unique and aromatic herb in their own gardens.
section_horticulture_planting: Dorema ammoniacum, also known as Levant dorema or Iranian fennel, is a perennial herb that requires specific planting conditions to thrive. When planting, choose a location with full sun and well-drained soil that is rich in organic matter. Sow the seeds in early spring or fall, about 1/4 inch deep and 12-18 inches apart, in rows that are 3 feet apart. Water the soil gently but thoroughly after sowing, and keep the soil consistently moist during the first growing season. As the plants grow, you can thin them out to 24-36 inches apart to allow for proper air circulation and sunlight penetration. It's essential to avoid overwatering, as this can lead to root rot and other problems. Additionally, Dorema ammoniacum prefers a slightly acidic to neutral soil pH, ranging from 6.0 to 7.5. With proper care and attention, these hardy plants will establish themselves quickly and provide you with an abundance of fragrant leaves and stems for years to come.
section_horticulture_caring: Dorema ammoniacum, also known as Chinese false chamomile or Ammianthium, is a perennial herb native to China and India that requires specific caring tips to thrive. To ensure optimal growth, it's essential to provide this plant with well-draining soil and full sun to partial shade. Watering should be done carefully, as the plant is sensitive to overwatering, which can lead to root rot. It's recommended to water deeply once or twice a week during spring and summer, allowing the top inch of soil to dry out between waterings. Fertilization can be done once a month with a balanced fertilizer, but avoid overfertilizing as this can cause burning of the leaves. Additionally, prune the plant regularly to maintain its shape and encourage new growth. It's also important to divide and replant every 3-4 years to prevent overcrowding and promote healthy root development. By following these caring tips, Dorema ammoniacum can thrive and provide a beautiful display of yellow flowers in the summer months.
section_horticulture_harvesting: Dorema ammoniacum, also known as Persian ginseng, is a perennial plant that requires careful harvesting to ensure its quality and sustainability. The best time for harvesting Dorema ammoniacum is during the fall season, when the leaves have turned yellow and the stems are dry. It's essential to harvest the plants early in the morning, after the dew has evaporated but before the heat of the day sets in. Use scissors or pruning shears to cut off the aerial parts of the plant, leaving a small portion of stem intact to ensure regeneration. Harvest only the top third to half of the plant, as this allows the remaining part to continue growing and producing new leaves. Avoid pulling out the entire plant, as this can damage the roots and reduce future yields. Finally, dry the harvested material thoroughly, either by air drying or using a machine, to prevent spoilage and maintain its quality for future use in traditional medicine, cooking, or cosmetics.
section_horticulture_pests_diseases: Dorema ammoniacum, also known as Persian sumac, is a deciduous shrub native to Iran, Turkey, and Afghanistan. Despite its medicinal and culinary uses, it can be susceptible to several pests and diseases that can affect its growth and yield. One of the most common pests affecting Dorema ammoniacum is the spider mite, which feeds on the sap of the plant causing yellowing or bronzing of leaves and fine webbing. Aphids are another significant pest, which secrete honeydew that attracts ants and promotes the growth of sooty mold. Fungal diseases such as leaf spot, caused by pathogens like Colletotrichum spp., can also infect Dorema ammoniacum, causing black spots or lesions on leaves and stems. Root rot caused by fungal pathogens like Rhizoctonia solani is another significant disease that can occur when the plant is waterlogged or in areas with poor drainage. Additionally, nematodes like Meloidogyne incognita can infest the roots of Dorema ammoniacum, causing stunted growth and reduced yields.
section_botany: Dorema ammoniacum, also known as Dorema and Chinese False Rue, is a perennial herbaceous plant that belongs to the family Apiaceae. Its botanical characteristics include a robust stem, typically growing up to 1-2 meters in height, with a glabrous surface and a distinctive onion-like aroma when bruised or crushed. The leaves of D. ammoniacum are compound, divided into three to five leaflets, which are elliptical in shape and have wavy margins, measuring between 10-20 centimeters in length. The flowers of the plant are small, yellowish-white in color, and arranged in umbels, with each umbel containing 5-7 individual flowers. The fruit is a schizocarp, composed of several mericarps that are oblong or elliptical in shape and have a distinctive ridged surface. Additionally, D. ammoniacum has a strong, characteristic odor, reminiscent of myrrh, which is released when the plant's parts are crushed or damaged, giving it its alternative name "Chinese False Rue".
section_botany_taxonomy: Dorema ammoniacum belongs to the family Apiaceae, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants commonly known as carrot or parsley families. Specifically, it falls under the genus Dorema, a group of perennial plants native to Western Asia and Eastern Europe. The species name ammoniacum refers to its characteristic strong odor, reminiscent of ammonia, which is due to the presence of volatile compounds in its leaves and stems. As a member of the Apiaceae family, Dorema ammoniacum shares similarities with other plants like carrots, parsley, and parsnips, but exhibits unique characteristics that distinguish it as a distinct species within this larger group.
section_botany_variants: Dorema ammoniacum has variants, such as Dorema ammoniacum subsp. ammoniacum and Dorema ammoniacum subsp. orientale. These subspecies differ in their geographical distribution, leaf shape, and seed size. The subsp. ammoniacum is found in central Asia, including Kazakhstan, Uzbekistan, and Turkmenistan, while the subsp. orientale is native to Iran and Afghanistan. Additionally, some sources recognize Dorema ammoniacum var. gracile, which is characterized by its slender stems and smaller leaves compared to the typical variety. These variants highlight the complexity and diversity within the species, reflecting the adaptations of Dorema ammoniacum to different environments and ecological niches.
section_botany_distribution: Dorema ammoniacum, also known as Iranian dorema or gum ammoniac, is native to Central Asia and the Middle East. Its geographic distribution ranges from Iran, Afghanistan, and Pakistan in the west to Xinjiang Province in China in the east, and includes parts of Turkey and Turkmenistan. The plant thrives in arid and semi-arid regions with low rainfall, typically growing in deserts, steppes, and mountainous areas up to an altitude of 2,500 meters above sea level.
section_botany_life_cycle: Dorema ammoniacum, also known as false rue or Iranian dorema, is an annual or biennial plant that follows a specific life cycle. It begins its life by germinating from seed in the spring or early summer, typically after a period of warm and moist weather. The plant grows into a rosette of leaves, which can reach up to 60 cm tall, before producing a flowering stem. After pollination, the plant produces fruit and seeds, which are dispersed by wind or animals, allowing it to propagate and begin its life cycle again.
section_history: Dorema ammoniacum, also known as Iranian celery or Persian lovage, has a rich history dating back thousands of years. The plant's seeds have been used in traditional medicine for their antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory properties, treating ailments such as toothaches, rheumatism, and respiratory issues. In ancient times, the seeds were also used as a natural remedy for skin conditions, including eczema and acne.
section_history_mythology: Dorema ammoniacum, also known as wild celery, has rich mythological references in ancient cultures. In Greek mythology, it is associated with the story of Ammon, a god from Libya who was said to have discovered the plant's medicinal properties. The plant's name "ammoniacum" is derived from Ammon's association with the herb. Additionally, the plant was also mentioned in Hindu mythology as one of the five nectars created by Lord Shiva during the churning of the ocean of milk.
section_history_simbology: Dorema ammoniacum, also known as Indian celery or false cardamom, holds significant symbolic meanings across various cultures. In ancient Greek and Roman medicine, it was associated with purification, cleansing, and protection from evil spirits. The plant's distinctive aroma is said to represent spiritual growth, enlightenment, and inner wisdom. Additionally, its use in traditional Ayurvedic medicine underscores the importance of balance and harmony in body and mind.
section_history_literature: Dorema ammoniacum, also known as gum ammoniac or Iranian myrrh, has a rich history that can be traced back to ancient civilizations. The earliest recorded mention of this herb is in the works of Greek physician Dioscorides (40-90 AD), who described its medicinal properties and uses. The Greek pharmacopeia and the Arabian medicinal texts, such as the Canon of Medicine by Avicenna (980-1037 AD), also mention Dorema ammoniacum for its therapeutic benefits.
section_history_artifacts: Dorema ammoniacum, a plant species native to Central Asia, has been an important part of human history and culture. Archaeological discoveries have revealed ancient trade routes along which Dorema ammoniacum seeds were transported as a valuable commodity. The plant's aromatic resin, known as asafoetida, was used in medicine, cooking, and perfumery. Historical artifacts such as Roman coins, Greek pottery, and Middle Eastern textiles often feature depictions of the plant, highlighting its significance in ancient commerce and daily life.
intro_desc: Dorema ammoniacum, commonly known as Iranian Galbanum, is a plant-based remedy that has been prized for its potent anti-inflammatory properties, which have been shown to improve overall health by reducing pain and inflammation in the body. The herb requires well-drained soil and full sun to thrive, and is typically propagated through seed or cuttings, making it a versatile and accessible plant for cultivation. Botanically speaking, Dorema ammoniacum is a member of the carrot family, and its resinous leaves and stems contain a complex blend of volatile oils that are responsible for its medicinal properties. For centuries, the herb has been referenced in traditional medicine, with ancient texts such as the Shahnameh of Ferdowsi and the Materia Medica of Dioscorides documenting its use for treating a range of ailments.
medicine_desc: Dorema ammoniacum, also known as Iranian galbanum, helps with various health issues including bronchitis, coughs, and respiratory problems due to its expectorant and anti-inflammatory properties. Its essential oil has been used in traditional medicine for centuries to ease pain and reduce fever. Iranian galbanum is also used in aromatherapy for its decongestant and antispasmodic effects. Additionally, it has been used as a diaphoretic to induce sweating and promote digestion. Its analgesic and anti-inflammatory properties make it a popular remedy for rheumatic and arthritic pain.

The active constituents of Iranian galbanum include borneol, bornyl acetate, and bornyl isobutyrate, which contribute to its medicinal properties. These compounds have been shown to exhibit expectorant, anti-inflammatory, and antispasmodic activities. The essential oil of Iranian galbanum is rich in sesquiterpenes, which are responsible for its analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects. The presence of these bioactive compounds is responsible for the plant's therapeutic effects.

The most commonly used parts of the Iranian galbanum plant for medicinal purposes are its resin, roots, and leaves. The resin, which is rich in essential oils, is often used to produce medicines for respiratory issues. The roots of the plant are used to prepare decoctions for treating various health conditions, including coughs and fever. The leaves of Iranian galbanum are also used in traditional medicine to ease pain and reduce inflammation.

Improper use of Iranian galbanum can lead to several side effects, including allergic reactions, digestive issues, and interactions with other medications. Prolonged use of the plant's essential oil can cause skin irritation, allergic reactions, and even anaphylaxis in some individuals. The plant's anti-inflammatory properties can also lead to interactions with other medications, such as blood thinners and diabetes medications.

To use Iranian galbanum medicinally safely, several precautions must be taken. Pregnant and breastfeeding women should avoid using the plant's essential oil due to its potential toxicity. Individuals with sensitive skin should perform a patch test before using the oil. It is also essential to consult a healthcare professional before using Iranian galbanum, especially if taking other medications or suffering from chronic health conditions.
horticulture_desc: Dorema ammoniacum, also known as Iranian Galbanum, grow in well-drained soils and full sun to partial shade. It thrives in dry to semi-arid regions with hot summers and cold winters. Temperature tolerance ranges from -20°C to 30°C. Soil pH tolerance is alkaline to neutral.

For optimal growth, plant Dorema ammoniacum seeds in autumn or spring. Space them 30-40 cm apart. Sow seeds 1-2 cm deep in a mixture of sand and soil. Water gently and maintain consistent moisture. Avoid overwatering, which can lead to root rot.

Dorema ammoniacum resin can be harvested after 4-5 years of growth. Harvest the resin by making a small incision on the stem. Collect the resin before it solidifies. Dry the resin in the sun or using machines to preserve its quality. Harvesting should be done carefully to avoid damaging the plant.

Dorema ammoniacum is susceptible to aphids, whiteflies, and spider mites. These pests can be controlled using insecticidal soap or neem oil. Root rot, caused by overwatering, can be treated by reducing water intake and improving drainage. Regular monitoring and pruning can help prevent disease and pest infestations.
botany_desc: Dorema ammoniacum, also known as Iranian Galbanum, is a perennial plant species in the carrot family (Apiaceae). It has a woody, taprooted stem with a height of 30-60 cm, bearing leaves that are divided into 2-3 leaflets, each 2-4 cm long and lance-shaped.

Taxonomically, Dorema ammoniacum belongs to the family Apiaceae, subfamily Apioideae, tribe Ferulieae, and is the sole species within the genus Dorema. Its binomial nomenclature is Dorema ammoniacum Boiss.

There are several variants of Dorema ammoniacum, including D. ammoniacum var. ammoniacum and D. ammoniacum var. glabrum. These variants can be distinguished based on differences in their leaf shape, size, and the presence or absence of pubescence.

Dorema ammoniacum is native to the mountainous regions of the Middle East, particularly in Iran, where it grows in alpine and subalpine zones. It has also been naturalized in some parts of the Caucasus and Central Asia.

The life cycle of Dorema ammoniacum is characterized by a dormant winter period, during which the taproot remains intact. In the spring, the taproot grows and produces leaves and stems, which eventually produce yellow flowers. After flowering, the plant produces small fruits, which disperse the seeds, allowing the cycle to begin anew.
history_desc: Dorema ammoniacum, also known as Iranian galbanum, is an ancient plant with a long history of use. Its essential oil has been used for centuries in traditional medicine, perfumery, and incense production. It was used in ancient Egypt, Greece, and Rome for its antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties.

In ancient Persian mythology, Dorema ammoniacum was associated with the mythical plant, Haoma, a sacred plant that granted immortality. The Greek philosopher, Herodotus, mentioned the plant's use in rituals and ceremonies.

In various cultures, Dorema ammoniacum has been a symbol of purification, protection, and spiritual growth. In Hinduism, it is associated with the goddess Lakshmi, representing spiritual growth and enlightenment.

Historical texts mention Dorema ammoniacum's use in ancient Greek medicine, as described in the works of Hippocrates and Galen. The plant's essential oil was used to treat a variety of ailments, including wounds and skin conditions.

Archaeological discoveries have revealed artifacts from ancient civilizations, showcasing the plant's significance. For example, a 4,000-year-old Persian vessel from the Elamite period was found containing Dorema ammoniacum essential oil.
